text: Hello, everyone. This is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California, back with an interesting video for the ETAP surgeons out there.
This is a video of a robotic ETAP reef stopa repair of a recurrent incisional hernia after a supposed robotic tap.
The surgeon inserts a 5mm optical trocar into the left upper quadrant to begin the procedure.
The surgeon navigates through scar tissue to successfully insert a second 8mm robotic port, enhancing access for the robotic ETAP reef stopa repair procedure.
The surgeon uses a spinal needle to determine the trajectory for port placement. During the insertion, a hole is inadvertently created in the posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum.
The surgeon attempts to create space during dissection with a laparoscope but encounters significant scarring, leading to the decision to abandon the space due to its disappearance.

The surgeon attempts a five millimeter optical entry into the right upper quadrant, targeting the right retroactive space, after deciding against placing a third port in the previous space due to discomfort.
The video shows the surgeon smoothly entering the left upper quadrant with a five millimeter optical trocar, reaching the areolar plane with minimal resistance.
During laparoscopic dissection, the surgeon encounters extensive scar tissue extending caudally and laterally. Unexpected anatomical features become visible amidst the scar tissue.
The video shows a gap in the posterior rectus sheath, which appears very thin. Transparent tissue is visible, providing a view into the abdominal cavity, which should not be seen laterally.
So immediately I realized that the previous tap operation probably violated the posterior rectus sheet.
The surgeon inserts an eight millimeter robotic port into the epigastrium, positioned just lateral to the left linea alba, to access the upper part of the incisional hernia.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic crossover maneuver to access the preperitoneal space, followed by entry into the left retroactive space. This allows for the placement of robot ports in both retroactive spaces, facilitating docking in the upper abdomen.

The surgeon uses a hook in the right lateral, right upper quadrant port while the laparoscopic scope is positioned in the epigastric port. A visible twitch of the left rectus muscle indicates proximity to the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon opens the left posterior rectus sheath, revealing the underlying left rectus muscle. The dissection progresses to expose the initial five millimeter port after further exploration.
The surgeon optically advances the port into the abdominal cavity to desufflate it, creating more space for entry into the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon places a robot port in the left upper quadrant, adjusting its position higher due to the initial placement being too low in the left rectus. The robotic instruments used include a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand and a monopolar curved scissor in the right hand.

The surgeon views the surgical field from a caudal perspective, identifying the right rectus muscle on the roof and the left rectus muscle on the opposite side. Dissection is performed in the left retroactive space, revealing significant scarring indicative of a previous surgical complication, which complicates the current ETAP operation.

The surgeon observes the posterior rectus sheath extending towards the linea alba, with both posterior rectus sheaths visible. The preperitoneal space is exposed, revealing some mesh. The dissection is performed from a top-down angle.

The surgeon dissects ventrally and dorsally to the posterior rectus sheath, visualizing both the preperitoneal and retrorectal spaces. The posterior rectus sheath is divided, ensuring no bowel is present on the preperitoneal side. The rectus muscle is carefully separated from the posterior rectus sheath, revealing mesh located in both the preperitoneal and retrorectus spaces.
The surgeon performs dissection in the left retrorectus space, encountering purple secure strap absorbable tackers amidst the tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects the rectus muscle from the posterior rectus sheath using cautery, ensuring shallow scoring of the tissue to avoid areas where the bowel may be adhered to the rectus muscle due to the absence of peritoneum or posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon applies downward traction with the left hand while using cautery on the roof to avoid bowel injury. The mesh is being carefully dissected from the diastatic linea alba, revealing both the dorsal and ventral sides of the posterior rectus sheath before division.

The surgeon maintains the mesh down in both the preperitoneal and retrorectal spaces. The posterior rectus sheath is visibly violated laterally in the retrorectal space. The surgeon explores laterally towards the linea semilunaris to identify a clearer dissection plane, moving from known to unknown areas, avoiding scar tissue.

The surgeon encounters distorted anatomy during the ETEP procedure, creating a hole in the peritoneum on the right side to inspect the abdominal cavity for bowel presence. Cauterization is performed primarily on the muscle side rather than the posterior rectus sheath or peritoneal side.

The surgeon continues the dissection by tapping on the visible mesh and carefully separating the posterior rectus muscle from it. They follow natural tissue planes while maintaining hemostasis. The CT scan indicates bowel herniation caudal to the mesh into the hernia sac.
The surgeon carefully dissects to reveal a new space, identifying omentum and avoiding bowel. The dissection reaches the caudal edge of the mesh, which is separated from the anterior fascia.

The surgeon navigates a challenging dissection, encountering muscle tissue unexpectedly medial to the linea alba. The posterior rectus sheath is being divided, revealing the complex anatomical relationships in this area.

The surgeon identifies that the muscle being touched by the scissors is actually rectus muscle, not the true linea alba. The retrorectus space is exposed, revealing denuded muscle and possibly neoperitoneum or shredded peritoneum fused to the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon cautiously dissects through the tissue, concerned about the potential presence of bowel on the other side. The dissection reveals muscle tissue, and the surgeon carefully removes the mesh while navigating through distorted anatomical planes.

The surgeon carefully removes mesh from the roof of the surgical field, revealing omentum and ensuring no bowel is adhered to the mesh. The hernia sac is visible on the right side of the screen, while muscle tissue is observed on the left. The posterior rectus sheath is fused to the rectus muscle, requiring careful dissection to separate these structures.
The surgeon removes tissue from the posterior rectus sheath, progressing past the previous dissection site and moving into the pelvic region.

The surgeon observes multiple purple tacks on the muscle while approaching the caudal edge of the defect. The anatomical relationship between the posterior rectus sheath and its insertion into the linea alba is noted as complex.

The surgeon removes mesh from the left rectus muscle, revealing omentum adhered to the hernia sac. The distorted anatomy requires careful navigation, with assistance to push down the omentum for better visibility.

The surgeon continues to remove mesh from the rectus muscle. The mesh appears to be adhered to the medial edge of the anterior fascia, possibly due to the previous surgeon dividing the posterior rectus sheath close to the linea alba.

The surgeon continues to remove mesh and the posterior rectus sheath from the rectus muscle, exposing the anterior surface of the mesh. The omentum is cleared from the ventral surface of the mesh, revealing its caudal edge, which is prepared to be sewn to the opposite edge.

The surgeon completes the separation of the mesh from the right anterior fascia and linea alba area, ensuring the posterior rectus sheath is prepared for closure. The edge of the mesh is positioned to be sewn to the left side later.

The surgeon continues dissection in the retroactive space, focusing on the plane where the fat accumulates, staying close to the posterior rectus sheath. The caudal edge is identified, and the surgeon scores the edge of the peritonealized tissue to locate the preperitoneal space in the midline.

The surgeon develops the preperitoneal space dorsal to the posterior rectus sheath, isolating the sheath on both the right and left sides. Fat is brought down in the space below the umbilicus, and scissors are visible in the retroactive space as the posterior rectus sheath is prepared for cutting.

The surgeon divides the attenuated posterior rectus sheath in the retro-rectus space, revealing the left rectus muscle with minimal diastasis. The division continues with the more robust portion of the posterior rectus sheath above the arcuate line.

The surgeon completes the caudal dissection in the retro-rectus space, ensuring good mesh overlap caudal to the defect. The attenuated posterior rectus sheath is divided, and the fat is kept elevated. A defect is visible, with the mesh laid out on the floor, and a small hole is identified that requires closure.

The surgeon measures the defect, documenting its dimensions as approximately seven by eleven or eight by eleven centimeters. The defect closure is shown, with the mesh sewn to the contralateral left posterior rectus sheath to close the floor and exclude the retroactive space from the peritoneal space. A large piece of mesh is placed with excellent overlap around the defect, without any fixation.
